he tree falls down unbroken and unbent, and without being
entangled in other trees, it will confer victory on the king. So will
one that falls with its tip towards the east or north. If it falls down
under contrary circumstances, it should be abandoned.
Four digits of the tree at the top and eight digits at the
bottom should be cut off, and then the trunk should be put into
water. After being taken out it should be carried to the city-gate
either through a carriage or by men.

Glory of IndrtCs Banner XLIII 349
?n5ft sm** i
If the spokes of the carriage were to be broken when the tree-
trunk is put on it or carried by it, the king's army would be shatter-
ed (i.e. there would be revolt), if the rim breaks, the army will be
destroyed; if the axle, the king's wealth would be lost; and if the
axle-pin gives way, the carpenter will come to grief.
